beats.prompt: Quick primer — what 2ⁿ and Σ mean before we build Pascal's triangle.

interaction.title: Exponents and Σ
interaction.body: 2³ means 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 — the exponent counts how many times you multiply. Σ (sigma) means 'sum over all the values below.' We'll use both in the binomial theorem.

feedback.correct: Good — 2ⁿ doubles each step; Σ sums them all.
hints: 2¹=2, 2²=4, 2³=8 — each is double the one before.
Σₖ f(k) means add f(k) for every k in the range.
Continue.

beats.prompt: Before we continue: match each expression to its value. These come from L1 and L2.

pairs.left: Sum of row n of Pascal's triangle

feedback.correct: Exactly — powers of two from L1 are Pascal's row sums in disguise.
hints: Tap a left item then its matching right item.
2⁰+2¹+2²+2³ = 1+2+4+8 = 15; each Pascal row doubles; C(n,k) = unordered-selection count.
Match all three pairs.

beats.prompt: Here's row 4 of Pascal's triangle:  1  4  6  4  1. Add it up — and guess the next row's total without building it.

25 — it's 5².note: That's n² thinking. Row totals double each step (1,2,4,8,16…) → next is 2⁵=32.

32 — it doubles.note: Yes — each row doubles: 16→32=2⁵. Setting a=b=1 makes every row sum 2ⁿ.

Can't tell without building it.note: It's not random — every row sums to 2ⁿ, so 16 doubles to 32.

beats.prompt: Expand (a+b)². What is the coefficient of the ab term?

feedback.correct: Right — 2 = C(2,1); the cross term shows up twice, ab+ba.
hints: Expand (a+b)(a+b) term by term.
Freshman's dream says a²+b², but it misses two middle terms: a·b and b·a.
a·b + b·a = 2ab → coefficient 2 = C(2,1).

beats.prompt: Track A: here's the full (a+b)² expansion — see how the coefficients come from C(2,k) before building the full triangle.

interaction.title: Why the coefficients are 1, 2, 1
interaction.body:
(a+b)² = (a+b)(a+b)
         = a·a + a·b + b·a + b·b
         = a²  +  2ab  +  b²

Coefficients: 1, 2, 1 = C(2,0), C(2,1), C(2,2).
These are row 2 of Pascal's triangle.

In general: the coefficient of aⁿ⁻ᵏbᵏ in (a+b)ⁿ is C(n,k) — the number of ways to pick which k of the n factors contribute the b.

feedback.correct: The cross terms are the key — they give C(n,k) counts.
hints: Notice 2 = C(2,1): two ways to pick one 'b' from two factors.
In (a+b)³ there are C(3,1)=3 ways to pick one b from three factors.
That's why the a²b coefficient is always C(n,1).

beats.prompt: Tap each cell to build Pascal's triangle row by row. Each cell is the sum of the two directly above it — watch the running total in the right margin.

feedback.correct: Every row doubles the last — the same 2ⁿ powers from L1, reappearing as Pascal's row sums.
hints: Tap any blank cell to reveal its value.
Each cell = sum of the two cells directly above it.
After a whole row is revealed the right margin shows its total as a power of 2.

beats.prompt: Three ways to read the same identity — tap each card to reveal.

lenses.label: Pascal's triangle cell (n,k)
lenses.body: Every cell = C(n,k), the same combination count from L2. Row 4's entries 1, 4, 6, 4, 1 are C(4,0) through C(4,4).

lenses.label: Binomial theorem at a=b=1
lenses.body: (a+b)ⁿ = Σₖ C(n,k) aⁿ⁻ᵏ bᵏ. Set a=b=1: Σₖ C(n,k) = 2ⁿ. That's exactly the row sum you just built.

lenses.label: Symmetry C(n,k) = C(n,n−k)
lenses.body: Choosing k objects in = choosing n−k objects out. The row mirrors: C(5,2) = C(5,3) = 10.

feedback.correct: Three routes, one identity: C(n,k) is Pascal's cell, the binomial coefficient, and the combination count.
hints: Reveal all three lens cards.
Setting a=b=1 in (a+b)ⁿ=ΣC(n,k)aⁿ⁻ᵏbᵏ gives ΣC(n,k)=2ⁿ — the row sum you built in l3-explore.
C(n,n−k)=C(n,k): choosing k items in is the same as choosing n−k items out.

beats.prompt: Is (1+√2)ⁿ + (1−√2)ⁿ always an integer? Type yes or no.
beats.interviewNote: GB p.36–37: (1+√2)¹⁰⁰+(1−√2)¹⁰⁰ is a positive integer I; since (1−√2)¹⁰⁰∈(0,1), floor((1+√2)¹⁰⁰) = I−1, so the 100th digit of (1+√2)¹⁰⁰ is 9.

feedback.correct: Right — the odd-power √2 terms pair off and cancel, leaving twice the even terms: an integer.
hints: Expand each binomial with the theorem and add the two expansions together.
k odd: C(n,k)(√2)ᵏ + C(n,k)(−√2)ᵏ = 0. The irrational pieces cancel in pairs.
k even: 2·C(n,k)·2^(k/2) ∈ ℤ. Sum of integers is an integer → yes.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: expand (a+10b)³ using the binomial theorem. What is the coefficient of the a²b term?

interaction.scenario: Any integer x can be written as a + 10b where a is its ones digit (0–9). Expanding (a+10b)³ via the binomial theorem lets you control the last digits of x³. Use row 3 of Pascal's triangle: 1, 3, 3, 1.

feedback.correct: Exactly — 30; row 3 is 1,3,3,1 and the b carries a 10: C(3,1)·a²·(10b) = 3·a²·10b = 30a²b.
hints: Write out four terms: Σ C(3,k) a^(3−k) (10b)^k for k=0,1,2,3.
The a²b term is k=1: C(3,1)·a²·(10b)¹ = 3·a²·10b.

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap — and the bridge to what comes next.

feedback.correct: Binomial theorem: (a+b)ⁿ = ΣC(n,k)aⁿ⁻ᵏbᵏ. Each row sums to 2ⁿ. The triangle is symmetric. Next: when sets overlap, subtraction enters — Inclusion–Exclusion (L4).
hints: (a+b)ⁿ = ΣC(n,k)aⁿ⁻ᵏbᵏ.
Row n sums to 2ⁿ (set a=b=1).
Next: |A∪B| = |A|+|B|−|A∩B|.
